title: The HBR Interview: Bob Sternfels on Shaping McKinsey's Next Century
content:
Since becoming McKinsey & Company's global managing partner in 2021, Bob Sternfels has navigated a series of thorny challenges. He has taken significant steps to restore the consulting firm's reputation following several scandals, most notably its role in the U.S. opioid crisis. To achieve this, he has worked to tighten the rules on client selection and governance without alienating the firm's partners. Additionally, to prepare McKinsey for the AI era, he is driving a major organizational transformation. This shift focuses less on traditional consulting services and more on delivering tangible, technology-driven outcomes.

In a recent interview reflecting on McKinsey's centennial, Sternfels noted that the half-life of companies is getting shorter as technological change quickens. He described this milestone as an opportunity to examine the firm's history and determine how it needs to adapt in an unpredictable world. While much attention is given to the technology itself, Sternfels emphasized that the real secret to success lies in organizational change. Companies must figure out how work looks after AI is implemented and whether a flatter organization can increase efficiency. When organizations successfully align these structural changes, business leaders like the CFO and CIO finally get on the same page.

Despite the promise of AI, Sternfels acknowledged that full integration is taking longer than many people initially anticipated. Internally, McKinsey is actively discussing how artificial intelligence will fundamentally shift the economics of the consulting industry. These conversations revolve around the potential impacts on overall headcount, service pricing, workforce productivity, and long-term profit margins. By successfully navigating these complex internal discussions, McKinsey hopes to set a strong precedent for its clients. Ultimately, the firm aims to prove that mastering AI-driven organizational design will be the key to surviving the next century of business.
